title: Understand: First Amendment: Religion—Establishment Clause
description: Check your understanding of the First Amendment's Establishment Clause with this set of multiple-choice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the Supreme Court’s ruling in *Engel v. Vitale* (1962)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Students could not form religious clubs in public schools.
School-sponsored prayer in public schools violated the Establishment Clause.
The legislature could ban religious head coverings in public schools.
Teachers were required to lead students in daily Bible study.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What issue did the Supreme Court address *Kennedy v. Bremerton School District* (2022)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: School dress codes and religious attire
Prayer by a public school football coach after a football game
The display of religious symbols in public school classrooms
The distribution of religious literature on school grounds

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the outcome of *Kennedy v. Bremerton School District* (2022)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Supreme Court ruled in favor of the school district, allowing teacher-led prayer in the classroom.
The Supreme Court upheld the rights of students to lead religious activities at school-sponsored events.
The Supreme Court declared that public school teachers could not engage in religious expression while on duty in the classroom.
The Supreme Court affirmed the right of a public school football coach to say a private prayer on the field after a football game.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What issue did the Supreme Court address in *Lynch v. Donnelly* (1984)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Public funding for religious schools
The display of religious symbols on public property
Mandatory prayer in public schools
Tax exemptions for religious organizations

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the Court’s decision in *Lynch v. Donnelly* (1984)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Public schools could not teach creationism alongside evolution in science classes.
The government could include holiday displays on public property without violating the Establishment Clause.
Religious organizations were exempt from certain taxes under the First Amendment.
Public schools were required to offer religious education as part of their curriculum.

assessment_items.question_content:
**In *Engel v. Vitale* (1962), the Supreme Court held that state-sponsored prayer in public schools violated the \_\_\_\_.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Commerce Clause
Full Faith and Credit Clause
Establishment Clause
Free Exercise Clause

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of these issues have been addressed by the Supreme Court’s interpretation of the Establishment Clause?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Government-sponsored prayer
Aid to religious education
Government-sponsored religious symbols
Religious teachings in Catholic schools
Birthright citizenship
An approach that the Supreme Court used to determine if a law violated the Establishment Clause
An experimental set of laws that Congress passed in the 1970s to test the limits of the Establishment Clause
An test used by states to determine how religious a candidate for the federal judiciary was before they took office
A screening used by Congress to determine whether a law violated the Free Exercise Clause of the First Amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
**In which Supreme Court case did the Court recognize that it had abandoned the *Lemon* test and decide to replace it with a history-and-tradition test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *Engel v. Vitale* (1962)
*Lemon v. Kurtzman* (1971)
*Lynch v. Donnelly* (1984)
*Kennedy v. Bremerton School District* (2022)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement best describes the Supreme Court’s involvement in the issue of the use of religious symbols on public property before 1980?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Supreme Court did not get involved in the issue.
The Supreme Court  banned all religious symbols.
The Supreme Court banned only non-Christian symbols.
The Supreme Court ruled that all symbols were allowed.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which Supreme Court case dealt with the issue of religious symbols on public property?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *Engel v. Vitale* (1962)
*Lemon v. Kurtzman* (1971)
*Lynch v. Donnelly* (1984)
*Kennedy v. Bremerton School District* (2022)

assessment_items.question_content:
**In the 1970s, what was the Supreme Court’s position on the government’s role in funding religious institutions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The government cannot aid religious institutions under any circumstances.
Religious institutions can receive government funding if it first goes to citizens.
Any publicly funded programs cannot exclude religious institutions.
There are no limits on the government's role in funding religious institutions.
